does: This paper conducts a comprehensive empirical investigation of parameter-efficient fine-tuning for preference alignment in large language models, spanning over 300 experiments across multiple dimensions. The study systematically varies three crucial axes: alignment datasets (HH-RLHF and BeaverTails), alignment techniques (supervised fine-tuning and Direct Preference Optimization), and model architectures (LLaMA-1, Vicuna, Mistral variants). The work empirically measures how these choices affect downstream performance metrics and reveals consistent trends, including cases where simpler supervised fine-tuning outperforms more sophisticated preference optimization methods, and demonstrates the importance of data informativeness for successful alignment outcomes.
connects: This work builds directly on the growing literature of parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods like LoRA and QLoRA that democratized LLM adaptation, and extends recent work on preference alignment including DPO as an alternative to reinforcement learning from human feedback. It addresses a recognized gap in the community regarding systematic understanding of alignment sensitivity to data quantity, quality, and method choice, extending beyond prior scattered observations. The paper responds to the emergence of multiple preference alignment datasets and techniques without clear guidance on their relative merits, connecting the practical concerns of practitioners to the theoretical foundations established by earlier work on instruction tuning and RLHF.
enables: This empirical characterization enables future researchers to make informed decisions about alignment technique selection based on their computational constraints and data availability. The systematic findings provide baselines and guidelines for practitioners designing alignment pipelines, informing whether to invest in expensive preference optimization or use simpler supervised fine-tuning. The work opens questions about when and why preference optimization fails relative to simpler approaches, potentially spurring theoretical investigation into alignment dynamics. These insights could guide the development of more efficient or robust alignment methods, and the experimental framework established could be extended to investigate emerging techniques, adapter architectures, or novel alignment objectives in parameter-efficient settings.
